judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit every expectation and are comparable; Report 2 is marginally more complete on decision-ownership and alternatives.

report_1.evidence: steps 1–5 were already done in April 2026 and reverted in production (commit 1712565)

report_2.evidence: it shows the migration was already done and reverted (commits 454a21f → 597777b → 1712565, ticket PLAT-31)

report_1.evidence: 1712565 (revert: "serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; /reports/monthly exhausted Postgres max_connections in production")

report_2.evidence: Commit 1712565 (Apr 21, 2026) reverts the Edgerunner deploy with the message: Edgerunner runs each request in a fresh isolate that cannot hold the SQLAlchemy pool; the per-request-connection stopgap (commit 597777b) opened connections faster than Postgres could recycle them, tripped `max_connections` ("FATAL: too many connections")

report_1.evidence: steps 1–5 were already done in April 2026 and reverted in production (commit 1712565)... Deploy, cut traffic over, retire the Cargobox service and `backend/Dockerfile`/`deploy/cargobox.yaml`.

report_2.evidence: Add an Edgerunner deploy config, e.g. `deploy/edgerunner.toml` ... replacing `deploy/cargobox.yaml` ... it shows the migration was already done and reverted

report_1.evidence: it should be re-opened explicitly with the platform/infra owner of PLAT-31 (and whoever owns the Postgres instance), framed as "the previous attempt failed on connection pooling..." ... Leadership should be told the migration was already tried and rolled back

report_2.evidence: The people who should be in the decision: whoever owns ticket PLAT-31 and executed the April revert (the platform team behind the PLAT-N scheme), the DBA/owner of the shared Postgres (`max_connections` was the failure point), and the leadership sponsor — who should be shown the revert commit so the cost-saving decision is made with knowledge of the prior failure.
